narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your background like your high family income bracket and solid LSAT score of 38, which stands above the typical student's score of around 37. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 41.1% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the tough decision of rejecting your application. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lowest group compared to peers. This outweighed your LSAT strength, which was the top factor pulling in the opposite direction toward predicting success, as it exceeded what most students achieve. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also far below the typical 5.7, added further weight toward the failure side, reinforcing concerns about consistent early performance. Positively, your undergraduate GPA of 2.8—though under the average 3.2—still nudged the prediction toward passing, much like your family's high income level, which the model sees as a supportive advantage not shared by most applicants. Being in the part-time work category, uncommon for the 93% who go full-time, provided a small boost toward success in the model's eyes. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having standout elements like LSAT and family support, but the low decile rankings in both early and later years dominated, placing you outside the stronger academic patterns that predict bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—it's just how these key metrics stacked up. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps aiming for full-time study next time, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. You're capable of turning this around, and many in similar situations have succeeded with targeted improvements. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
